coredata.dc:title: Heat dissipation performance research of battery modules based on composite phase change materials cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huanhuan
Yuan, Jifeng
Yang, Zhiyin
Gu, Zhengjian
Wang, Yaping
Wang, Tiansi
Bao, Jun
Yang, Tao
Pei, Lei
Jiang, Haobin
Yuan, Chaochun
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) performance and increase cycle life, it is crucial to design a reliable and efficient battery thermal management system (BTMS). Compared with other active battery cooling methods, passive phase change material (PCM) cooling has the advantages of simple structure, good temperature control uniformity, strong reliability, and no additional energy consumption. This article uses a series of experimental procedures to add expanded graphite to traditional paraffin wax PCM and obtain composite phase change materials (CPCM) with different mixing ratios. A control experiment is created to compare the cooling performance of the CPCM cooling with natural convection cooling, research shows that the cooling performance of CPCM can well meet the cooling demand which maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of battery modules. In addition, to study the performance of thermal properties parameters related to CPCM, a COMSOL numerical calculation model of battery module based on CPCM cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model is established. The effectiveness and accuracy of the model are verified through heat generation experiments at different rates (0.5 C, 1 C, 1.5 C), with a maximum temperature error of 1.6 K and an error accuracy of less than 0.6%. Subsequently, the performance of phase change temperature, thermal conductivity and phase change latent heat is studied and reasonable value ranges are obtained, which are 312 K∼318 K, 2.5 W /(m·K), and 175 J/g∼200 J/g, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Battery thermal management system
Composite phase change materials
Electrochemical thermal coupling model
Liquid phase ratio

Numerical calculation model of the CPCM
Heat dissipation performance of the CPCM
Verification of the CPCM numerical calculation model
Heat dissipation effect of the CPCM thermal properties parameters
Effect of phase change temperature on heat dissipation performance
Effect of thermal conductivity on heat dissipation
Effect of latent heat of phase change on heat dissipation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical map of expandable graphite and expanded graphite.
EG/PW composite sample.
SEM images of EG and EG(10)/PW(90) composites: (a) EG× 300; (b) EG× 1000; (c) EG(10)/PW(90)× 300; (d)) EG(10)/PW(90)× 1000.
DSC curves of PW and EG/PW composites.
(a)Geometric model of battery module based on phase change cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model. (b)Mesh division of battery module.

(a) Battery module filled with composite phase change material (CPCM). (b) Battery module without CPCM. (c) Distribution of battery module temperature collection points. (d) The surface temperature of each battery when the battery module is discharged at 1 C at an ambient temperature of 298.15 K.
The maximum temperature of the battery module under different ambient temperatures: (a) 0.5 C; (b) 1 C; (c) 1.5 C.
Maximum temperature difference (∆Tmax) of battery modules under different ambient temperatures: (a) 0.5 C; (b) 1 C; (c) 1.5 C.
Comparison of experimental and simulated values of battery module temperature. (a) T = 283.15 K.(b) T = 283.15 K.(c) T = 298.15 K.(d) T = 298.15 K.(e) T = 308.15 K. (f)T = 308.15 K.
PCM liquid phase ratios at different PCM phase transition temperatures at the end of battery discharge.
Influence of phase transition temperature on battery module maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference.
Liquid phase ratio of PCM under different thermal conductivity at the end of battery module discharge.
Influence of thermal conductivity on battery module temperature.
Cloud diagram of temperature distribution under different latent heat of phase transition at the end of the battery module discharge.
Liquid phase ratio of PCM under different latent heat of phase transition.
The main instruments of the experiment.
Thermal conductivity of EG/PW composite phase change materials with different EG contents.
Phase transition temperature and latent heat of phase transition of different EG contents.
Thermophysical parameters of single battery.
Thermophysical parameters of composite phase change materials.
Maximum error (K) of experimental temperature data and simulated temperature data.

head.ce:title: Heat dissipation performance research of battery modules based on composite phase change materials cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) performance and increase cycle life, it is crucial to design a reliable and efficient battery thermal management system (BTMS). Compared with other active battery cooling methods, passive phase change material (PCM) cooling has the advantages of simple structure, good temperature control uniformity, strong reliability, and no additional energy consumption. This article uses a series of experimental procedures to add expanded graphite to traditional paraffin wax PCM and obtain composite phase change materials (CPCM) with different mixing ratios. A control experiment is created to compare the cooling performance of the CPCM cooling with natural convection cooling, research shows that the cooling performance of CPCM can well meet the cooling demand which maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of battery modules. In addition, to study the performance of thermal properties parameters related to CPCM, a COMSOL numerical calculation model of battery module based on CPCM cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model is established. The effectiveness and accuracy of the model are verified through heat generation experiments at different rates (0.5 C, 1 C, 1.5 C), with a maximum temperature error of 1.6 K and an error accuracy of less than 0.6%. Subsequently, the performance of phase change temperature, thermal conductivity and phase change latent heat is studied and reasonable value ranges are obtained, which are 312 K∼318 K, 2.5 W /(m·K), and 175 J/g∼200 J/g, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) pack is composed of many single battery series and parallel, the environment is harsh or large rate discharge will make the battery temperature rise sharply and the temperature difference increases, especially under extreme conditions, which may cause serious safety problems such as spontaneous combustion and explosion [1], with the passage of time, the inconsistent performance between the battery pack is more obvious, which accelerate battery aging. In general, the requirements of the battery thermal management system (BTMS) are to ensure that the maximum temperature of the battery module during charging and discharging does not exceed 50 °C, and the maximum temperature difference is not greater than 5 °C [2]. Therefore, in order to improve battery performance and increase cycle life, it is crucial to design a reliable and efficient battery thermal management system [3]. At present, the cooling methods of batteries mainly include air cooling, liquid cooling, phase change material cooling and heat pipe cooling [4]. Compared with other active battery cooling methods, passive phase change material (PCM) cooling has the advantages of simple structure, good temperature control uniformity, strong reliability, and no additional energy consumption [5,6].

Phase change materials are widely used in BTMS of power batteries, heat dissipation of electronic devices [7,8], solar energy storage [9,10], thermal insulation walls of building enclosures [11] and other fields due to their high latent heat and stable properties before and after phase change. Al Hallaj et al. [12] first proposed the application of PCM to BTMS. Sabbah et al. [13] conducted a comparative study on PCM cooling and air cooling, and found that under the same conditions, PCM cooling had a better heat dissipation effect on battery packs. Kizilel et al. [14] added graphite material to the PCM, and found that the thermal conductivity increased, and its heat dissipation effect is better than that of air cooling. Ling et al. [15] found that the composite phase change materials (CPCM) prepared by expanded graphite and paraffin can significantly reduce the temperature difference between single batteries and make the temperature distribution of battery packs more uniform. He et al. [16] prepared the expanded graphite/copper foam secondary skeleton material and found that it could control the maximum temperature of battery pack at 48 ℃ and the maximum temperature difference is 3.9 °C.

Because of its high latent heat, safety, non-toxicity and stable chemical properties, industrial paraffin is often used in the research of phase change materials. However, the thermal conductivity of paraffin wax is small, it is difficult to absorb the heat generated when the battery is charged and discharged in time, so it is necessary to improve the thermal conductivity of paraffin wax, and the common practice is to add materials with high thermal conductivity, such as carbon materials, metal materials and nanomaterials.

Aiming at the defect of low thermal conductivity of paraffin wax, the thermal conductivity of paraffin wax is improved by adding expanded graphite with high thermal conductivity carbon material in this work, and CPCM with different mass ratios are prepared by physical mixing method. The thermal properties of the prepared composite phase change materials are measured. In order to further verify the effect of heat dissipation and temperature control of phase change materials, a phase change cooling experiment of battery module is set up, based on the more accurate battery electrochemical model [17], the COMSOL numerical calculation model for modular batteries is established, and the cooling performance of CPCM related thermal properties parameters is studied.

In this work, the expanded graphite (EG)/paraffin wax (PW) composite phase change material is prepared by physical mixing method. The main raw materials used in this experiment are paraffin wax (model number 42#) and expandable graphite (particle size 50 mesh). The expanded graphite used in the experiment can be obtained from the raw material expandable graphite at high temperature expansion. The preparation process is as follows: (1) The expandable graphite is put into a vacuum drying oven and dried at a constant temperature of 60 °C for 24 h. (2) A small amount of expandable graphite is loaded with a crucible which is puffed into the Muffle furnace at 800 °C for 60 s, and the required expanded graphite is obtained. Expandable graphite and expanded graphite are shown in Fig. 1. It can be seen from the figure that expanded graphite has a porous structure and the morphology is wormlike, so it is also called worm graphite.

There are two main methods for preparing CPCM: direct impregnation method and vacuum impregnation method [18]. Direct impregnation method is simple to operate, EG is added to the molten PW in proportion to mix impregnation; The vacuum impregnation method has more vacuum conditions than the direct impregnation method, which can avoid the influence of air and make the impregnation more thorough. In this experiment, the vacuum impregnation method is adopted. Firstly, paraffin wax and expanded graphite are weighed by an electronic balance in a certain proportion and added to a beaker for initial stirring. Then, the fully stirred mixture is placed in a vacuum drying oven, and vacuum impregnation at 80 °C for 5 h to allow the paraffin to be fully adsorbed by expanded graphite. Selected EG/PW ratio as follows: the proportion of EG(4) /PW (96), EG (7)/PW (93), EG (10)/PW (90), EG (13)/PW (87). The main instruments used in the experiment are shown in Table 1.

The EG/PW mixture is placed in a stainless-steel mold (diameter 5 cm), moulded by a tablet press at 10 Mpa, then demoulded and polished to obtain the sample. Fig. 2 shows the optical image of the EG/PW composite sample. It can be seen that as the proportion of EG increases, the color of EG/PW composite phase change materials deepens. This is because as the amount of expanded graphite increases, it can adsorb more paraffin, allowing all paraffin to be adsorbed into the porous structure of expanded graphite.

Fig. 3 is the SEM image of EG (10)/PW (90) composite material. It can be seen from the figure that EG has a loose porous structure. After mixing with PW, PW is sucked into the pores, and the EG adsorbed with PW still retains its original worm like shape. However, it can be seen from the figure that a large number of pore structures in EG (10)/PW (90) composite material disappear, which is due to the filling of the pore structure by PW.

This article uses a thermal conductivity meter to measure the thermal conductivity of CPCM. The measurement method of this instrument is the transient hot wire method, and its testing principle is to use the measured hot wire resistance to infer the thermal conductivity. When measuring, place the hot wire of the temperature sensor of the thermal conductivity meter between two equally proportional phase change material samples, and press the 500 g weight. Wait until the temperature difference between the phase change material and the hot wire is less than 0.01 K before starting the test. In order to reduce experimental errors, each group of samples is measured 5 times, and the average value is taken as the final thermal conductivity coefficient value.

Table 2 shows the thermal conductivity of EG/PW composite phase change materials with different EG mass contents. As shown in the table, with the increase of EG content, the thermal conductivity of composite phase change materials increases. This indicates that the addition of EG can effectively improve the thermal conductivity of the CPCM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent heat characterization of phase transition
ce:section.ce:para: The phase change behavior of phase change materials is mainly reflected by two parameters: phase change temperature and phase change latent heat. The value can be measured by Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) [19]. In this paper, the phase change temperature and latent heat of CPCM are measured by DSC thermal analysis with DSC25 instrument of TA Company. Test conditions: The sample is heated at 5 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere, and the test temperature range is 25–70 °C(Temperature accuracy:±0.02 ℃). Fig. 4 shows the DSC curve of PW and EG/PW composites. The negative heat flow in the figure indicates that the sample absorbs heat during the heating process. As can be seen from the figure, compared with the DSC curve of PW, the peak position of the DSC curve of EG/PW composite material changes, and the DSC curve shifts to the left as the content of EG increases, but the phase transition behavior of EG/PW composite phase change material has the same characteristics as that of PW on the whole. In order to observe the phase change behavior of phase change materials, the peak temperature of EG/PW composite phase change materials is selected as its phase change temperature.

Table 3 shows the phase change temperature and phase change latent heat with different EG contents. It can be seen that the latent heat of phase change of EG/PW composite phase change materials decreases with the increase of EG contents, and the phase change latent heat decreases significantly. This is because EG has no latent heat of phase transition, and the latent heat of phase transition of EG/PW composites mainly comes from PW. When the content of EG increases, the proportion of PW decreases, so the latent heat is also decreasing.

As mentioned above, the analysis of the thermal properties test results of the prepared EG/PW composite phase change material shows that adding EG to PW can improve the thermal conductivity of the CPCM, and the more EG content, the higher the thermal conductivity of the EG/PW composite phase change material. However, an increase in EG content will reduce its latent heat of phase change. Therefore, this article comprehensively considers both latent heat and thermal conductivity, and selects EG (10)/PW(90) as the CPCM for the final cooling battery. Its thermal conductivity of 2.5 W/(m·K) is 12 times higher than the original pure PW's thermal conductivity of 0.21 W/(m·K).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical calculation model of the CPCM
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the thermal physical property parameters of CPCM will affect the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, this paper will study the influence of relevant thermal physical property parameters of CPCM (phase change temperature, thermal conductivity, phase change latent heat) on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, so as to provide practical reference for the future development prospects of the application of phase change materials in BTMS. However, if the laboratory preparation of CPCM with different parameter proportions, different phase change temperatures, and different phase change latent heat to study the relevant properties, the cost is huge and the research period is long, if the computer numerical calculation method can effectively solve this problem. Therefore, in this section, a 3D simulation model of CPCM is established.

In order to further study the influence of thermal physical parameters of CPCM on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, this paper set up a COMSOL Multiphysics numerical calculation model of battery modules composed of 6 single batteries in series. In our previous work, we validated the effectiveness and accuracy of the electrochemical and thermal coupling model for single batteries [20,21]. The electrochemical thermal coupling model is adopted for single batteries now, and the prepared EG/PW composite phase change materials are filled in the gap between batteries. The height of the CPCM is the same as that of the battery, and the three-dimensional geometric model of the battery module drawn is shown in Fig. 5(a). The PCM cooling model of battery module is composed of battery module and PCM module, On the basis of guaranteeing the grid quality, free tetrahedral mesh is used to divide the phase change cooling model of the battery module, as shown in Fig. 5(b).

Phase change materials will absorb heat or release heat during the phase change process, and there will be three phase regions in turn: solid phase region, paste region and liquid phase region. The paste region refers to the transition phase region between the solid phase and the liquid phase. In order to facilitate the study, the liquid phase rate θ is introduced to describe the solid phase region (θ = 0), the paste region (0 <θ < 1) and the liquid phase region (θ = 1), the equation expression [19] is as follows:
(1)
ρ = ( 1 − θ )
ρ
1
+ θ
ρ
2
(2)
C
p
=
1 ρ
(
( 1 − θ )
ρ
1
C
p , 1
+ θ
ρ
2
C
p , 2
)
+
L
1 → 2
∂
α
m
∂ T
(3)
k = ( 1 − θ )
k
1
+ θ
k
2
Where, subscripts 1 and 2 represent the solid phase and the liquid phase respectively, ρ is the density, kg/m3; C
p is the specific heat capacity, J/(kg·K); k is the thermal conductivity, W/(m·K); L
1→2 is the latent heat from phase 1 to phase 2, that is the latent heat of phase transition, J/kg; θ is expressed as the liquid phase rate in the phase change process of the phase change material, that is:
(4)
θ =
{
0

Where, T is the phase transition temperature, K; T
s and T
l are the lower limit temperature and upper limit temperature of the phase transition interval, K. In addition, the specific heat capacity is not only affected by the liquid phase rate, but also by the latent heat of phase transition L
1→2 . The α
m is used to describe the effect of L
1→2 on C
p , which is equivalent to the mass fraction:
(5)

The thermal property parameters of the battery include density, specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity. The interior of the battery is mainly composed of positive and negative collector, positive and negative electrode, diaphragm and other structures, and its thermal physical parameters can be calculated by weighting the thermal physical parameters of each structure, the specific calculation formula is as follows:
(6)
ρ =
∑
ρ
i
⋅
V
i
∑
V
i
(7)
C
p
=
∑
c
p i
⋅
V

i
(8)
K
r

∑

i

L

i
(9)
K

L

∑
L

K
i
Where, ρ
i is the density of each structure, V
i is the volume of each structure, c
pi is the specific heat capacity of each structure, L
i is the thickness of each structure, K
i is the thermal conductivity of each structure, the thermal conductivity inside the battery is divided into radial thermal conductivity K
r (parallel to the unit layer) and axial thermal conductivity K
h (perpendicular to the unit layer). According to the above formula, the thermal physical property parameters of the electrochemical thermal coupling model can be obtained, as shown in Table 4.
The specific heat capacity of EG(10)/PW(90) CPCM is measured by DSC sapphire method with NETZSCH DSC 200F3. The specific thermophysical parameters are shown in Table 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat dissipation performance of the CPCM
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method and process of CPCM have been mentioned in Section 2.2. The expanded graphite and paraffin wax are mixed in a certain proportion, and after heat treatment, EG/PW composite phase change materials are obtained, and then filled into the gap between the battery modules, and the box of the battery modules is made of acrylic material. The assembled battery pack module is shown in Fig. 6(a). A layer of thermal grease is applied between the CPCM and the battery to reduce the contact thermal resistance between the battery and the CPCM. In addition, in order to accurately obtain the temperature of the battery module, a thermocouple is arranged in the middle of each single battery to collect the surface temperature of the battery. In order to further verify the actual heat dissipation effect of CPCM applied to battery modules, set of battery modules without phase change materials are assembled and subjected to constant current discharge under natural cooling conditions, as shown in Fig. 6(b).

The battery test system used in the battery module experiment is NBT30V100AC (Test temperature limit:−40–150 ℃;Accuracy:0.1 ℃), measuring the maximum voltage of 30 V and the maximum current of 100A. The battery used in this experiment is square ternary LIB with capacity of 50Ah，the specific battery module test steps are as follows:
(1)
Constant Current method is used to charge the battery module. First, 1/3 C constant current is used to charge the battery until the voltage of a single battery reaches the cut-off voltage of 4.2 V, and then 1/20 C small current is used to charge it at constant current until the voltage of a single battery reaches 4.2 V again, so that the battery module is fully charged.
(2)
After full charge, set aside for one hour, so that the battery reaches stability, and then carry out constant discharge of the battery module, and continue to collect the temperature of each single battery during the discharge process of the battery module through the thermocouple.
(3)
After the end of discharge, adjust the temperature of the thermostat again to continue the module test of the next specified temperature. It should be noted that after adjusting the temperature of the thermostat, the battery temperature should be maintained at the same time as the ambient temperature before continuing to start the next round of module test. In order to facilitate the study of the temperature distribution of the battery module, we numbered each battery unit, as shown in Fig. 6(c).

The temperature values of each battery discharged at 1 C at an ambient temperature of 298.15 K are analyzed, as shown in Fig. 6(d). It can be seen from the figure that the temperature of each battery in the module is basically the same at the beginning, but with the increase of discharge time, the temperature difference of each battery is gradually obvious. Among them, T3 and T4 have the highest temperature, and T1 and T6 have the lowest temperature, which indicates that the highest temperature in the middle and the lowest temperature on both sides. In the following study, the temperature of T4 is used to represent the maximum temperature, and the temperature difference between T4 and T1 represents the maximum temperature difference ( ∆ Tmax) of the module.

In order to study the effect of the prepared CPCM on the heat dissipation performance of the battery module, the battery module with phase change material is compared with the battery module without CPCM(natural cooling). The test environment temperature and charge and discharge process of the two groups of experiments are the same. The battery module of natural cooling mode is named Pack1, and the battery module of phase change cooling mode is named Pack2. Fig. 7 shows the maximum temperature curve of the battery module of Pack1 and Pack2 at different ambient temperatures. It can be seen from the figure that the maximum temperature curve of the Pack2 battery module is lower than that of the Pack1, indicating that the phase change material dissipates heat for the battery, that is the addition of the CPCM effectively controls the increase in the temperature of the battery module.

Compared to Pack1 naturally cooling battery modules, the higher the ambient temperature at the same rate, the more the temperature of the Pack2 battery modules dropped. It can be seen from the Fig. 7 that when the discharge rate of the battery module is larger, the temperature curve of the phase-change cooling battery module drops more significantly, indicating that the latent heat of the phase-change material plays a role and effectively absorbs the battery heat. When the ambient temperature is 308.15 K, the maximum temperature of the battery module at the end of 1.5 C discharge under natural cooling conditions is 328.95 K, exceeding the maximum temperature required by the battery thermal management system, and the maximum temperature of the battery module after adding the CPCM is reduced to 320.85 K, meeting the requirements of battery thermal management [2]. The results show that under the condition of high discharge rate, the CPCM can control the temperature of the battery module in a reasonable range.

In addition to the maximum temperature of the battery module, the maximum temperature difference of the module is also another indicator, the temperature difference of the battery module will affect the battery performance. Fig. 8 shows the maximum temperature difference curve of battery modules of Pack1 and Pack2 at different ambient temperatures. The curve in the figure has obvious jitter, which is caused by the floating error of the temperature sensor, but it does not affect the overall trend of the curve. It can be seen that the maximum temperature difference curve of the Pack2 battery module is lower than that of the Pack1 battery module, indicating that the phase change material has a good temperature control effect on the battery module, that is the addition of CPCM effectively improves the temperature uniformity of the battery module. Under the same ambient temperature condition, it can be seen from the figure that the temperature difference of the battery module increases with the increase of discharge rate. As can be seen from the figure, the temperature difference of the battery module is basically 0 at the beginning, but with the increase of discharge time, the temperature difference of the module also increases, because the battery produces less heat at the beginning, and the increase of discharge time causes the heat accumulation in the middle of the module and cannot be dispersed in time, so the temperature difference gradually increases.

It is worth noting that by observing the three figures, it can be found that when the ambient temperature is 308.15 K, the maximum temperature difference curve of the battery module Pack2 added with the CPCM is almost a horizontal line, which is because the ambient temperature is high, the temperature of the battery module is easy to rise to the phase change temperature of the CPCM, and the latent heat of phase change plays its heat-absorbing role earlier. The heat of the battery module at low temperature is mainly absorbed by the sensible heat of the CPCM. However, it can be seen from Fig. 8(c) that when the ambient temperature is 308.15 K and the discharge rate is 1.5 C, the maximum temperature difference curve of Pack2 suddenly rises sharply at the end of discharge, because the CPCM in the middle of the battery module have all melted at this time, resulting in a sharp increase in the temperature of the intermediate battery. When the ambient temperature is 298.15 K and the discharge rate is 1.5 C, the maximum temperature difference curve of Pack2 suddenly shows a downward inflection point at the end of discharge. This is mainly because the temperature of the battery reaches the phase transition temperature of the CPCM at this time, and the latent heat of the phase change material begins to play its heat-absorbing role, so the temperature rise of the battery can be well controlled. Thus, the temperature difference between the battery modules is reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the CPCM numerical calculation model
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the reliability and accuracy of the battery modules based on CPCM and electrochemical thermal coupling model, the experimental results of the battery module are compared with the simulation results. Since the box of the battery module in the experiment is made of acrylic material, the outer layer of the battery module is set as heat insulation in the simulation according to the thermal conductivity of the acrylic material. The upper end of the module is in contact with the air, and the heat transfer condition of the wall in contact with the air is set as convective heat transfer, and the heat transfer coefficient is 7 W/(m2·K). It is known from Fig. 5(d) that No. 1 and No. 4 cells have the maximum flow temperature difference between battery packs, which can better represent the temperature rise of the entire battery module. Therefore, the experimental curves of T1 and T4 temperature collection points at different ambient temperatures and different magnifications are selected to compare with the simulation curves, as shown in Fig. 9.

As can be seen from Fig. 9, during the discharge process of the battery module, the experimental temperature curve and the simulation temperature curve change in the same trend, but there are some errors between the two, which may be caused by the following reasons: The model has been simplified, while the actual battery is more complicated; When the CPCM is filled with modules, the non-uniformity phenomenon is inevitable, and the thermal property parameters also have some experimental errors. The contact between battery and CPCM may produce thermal resistance. The temperature in the thermostatic chamber fluctuates, leading to some differences in convective heat transfer.

The absolute value of the maximum error of the experimental temperature and simulation temperature of No. 1 and No. 4 batteries is shown in Table 6. It can be seen that the maximum deviation between the experimental temperature and the simulation temperature is less than 1.7 K, and the error is less than 0.6%, which fully proves the reliability and accuracy of the battery modules based on CPCM and electrochemical thermal coupling model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat dissipation effect of the CPCM thermal properties parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy of the CPCM numerical model is verified through relevant experiments, then, based on the simulation model, the maximum temperature, maximum temperature difference and PCM liquid phase rate of the battery module during discharge are monitored under the conditions of ambient temperature of 308.15 K and discharge rate of 1.5 C. The heat dissipation effect of CPCM on battery modules under different phase change temperature, thermal conductivity and latent heat is studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of phase change temperature on heat dissipation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The phase transition temperature of PCM determines the time when PCM begins to melt [19]. In order to study the effect of phase transition temperature on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, five phase transition temperatures of 309 K, 312 K, 315 K, 318 K and 321 K are selected for simulation research within the temperature range allowed by the BTMS.

Fig. 10 describes the melting of PCM at the end of battery module discharge. It can be seen that the smaller the phase transition temperature is, the larger the liquid phase rate of PCM at the end of discharge, indicating that PCM with a smaller phase transition temperature begins to melt earlier in the battery module discharge process. Fig. 11 shows the temperature change curve of the battery module under different phase change temperatures. As can be seen from Fig. 11(a), increasing the phase transition temperature of PCM will increase the maximum temperature of the battery module, because the smaller the phase transition temperature of PCM is, the earlier the melting time will be, and the earlier the latent heat of PCM will play a role, resulting in a lower temperature of the battery module. However, the smaller the phase transition temperature of PCM, it means that the full melting time of PCM is too early, and when the PCM is completely melted, the battery module temperature will rise rapidly, resulting in a higher temperature at the end of the discharge. The maximum temperature of the battery module with phase transition temperature of 309 K is the smallest in the early stage of discharge, but with the discharge the temperature rise rate of the battery module is increasing, and the maximum temperature of the battery module at the end of discharge is higher than the maximum temperature of the battery module at 321 K. This is because the PCM with a small phase transition temperature has all melted in the late stage of discharge and can not longer absorb the heat of the battery module, resulting in a rapid rise in the temperature of the module.

As can be seen from Fig. 11(b), increasing the phase transition temperature will reduce the maximum temperature difference of the battery module. The maximum temperature difference of the battery module with a phase transition temperature of 309 K shows a descending inflection point at the later stage of discharge, which is because the PCM around the battery module is all melted at the inflection point, and the battery module can only dissipate heat through sensible heat of PCM, thus narrowing the gap between the lowest temperature and the highest temperature of the battery module. As a result, the temperature difference curve of the battery module drops.

In summary, the effect of phase change temperature on the heat dissipation of battery modules can be obtained. Too high or too low CPCM phase transition temperature is inappropriate. If the phase transition temperature is too high, the latent heat of phase transition will play late, and the temperature platform of the battery module will be at a higher temperature position. However, too low phase transition temperature will lead to premature melting time of PCM, and the highest temperature at the end of discharge is very high. Therefore, it can be concluded from the above discussion that the phase transition temperature of PCM is 312 K∼318 K [22,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of thermal conductivity on heat dissipation
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal conductivity of PCM determines its heat transfer capacity. In order to explore the influence of thermal conductivity of PCM on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, Selected the 0.2 W/(m K), 1 W/(m K), 2.5 W/(m K), 5 W/(m K), 8 W/(m K), 12 W/(m K) six is developed for the calculation of the coefficient of thermal conductivity.

Fig. 12 describes the melting of PCM at the end of battery module discharge. It can be seen that with the increase of thermal conductivity, the liquid phase rate of PCM at the end of battery module discharge also increases, indicating that the melting speed of PCM accelerates with the increase of thermal conductivity. This is mainly because improving the thermal conductivity of PCM will enhance its heat transfer capacity, and the heat generated by the battery module will be rapidly transferred to the surrounding PCM, so PCM can quickly and fully absorb the heat of the battery, thereby speeding up the melting of PCM.

Fig. 13 shows the temperature change curve of the battery module under different thermal conductivity. By observing Fig. 13(a), it can be found that PCM has not melted in the early discharge stage of the battery module, and the maximum temperature curves of the battery module under different thermal conductivity basically coincide. Since the battery module temperature increases with the discharge, when the battery temperature reaches the PCM melting temperature, the PCM latent heat starts to take effect, so the battery module temperature rise can be effectively controlled. However, at higher thermal conductivity, the temperature platform of the battery module is lower, mainly due to the greater thermal conductivity of the high thermal conductivity PCM, which can spread the heat of the battery module in time and absorb it faster. It is worth noting that when the thermal conductivity coefficient is greater than 2.5 W/(m·K), increasing the thermal conductivity coefficient has a minimal decrease in the maximum temperature of the battery module.

It can be seen from Fig. 13(b) that the maximum temperature difference of the module decreases with the increase of the thermal conductivity, mainly because the heat transfer ability of PCM with high thermal conductivity can rapidly spread the heat in the middle of the battery module to the surrounding area, effectively control the temperature rise of the intermediate battery, and reduce the temperature difference between the middle battery of the module and the batteries on both sides, thus reducing the temperature difference of the module. However, when the thermal conductivity is 0.2 W /(m·K), the maximum temperature difference trend of the battery module is different from the others, and the temperature difference of the battery module is the smallest of several thermal conductivity in the early stage of discharge, and it rises rapidly in the late stage of discharge. This is because the PCM with a small thermal conductivity can not spread the heat in the middle of the battery module to the surrounding area in time, so that the temperature of the middle single battery reaches the melting temperature of PCM earlier. At this time, the heat of the middle single battery of the module is mainly absorbed by the latent heat of PCM, and the temperature increase of the middle battery is small, and the temperature rise of the peripheral battery is originally smaller than the temperature rise of the middle battery, so the temperature difference of the battery module in the early discharge stage is relatively small. However, when the PCM in the middle of the battery module is all melted, the temperature difference of the module will appear a rapid rise.

Based on the above analysis, it can be concluded that the influence of thermal conductivity on the heat dissipation of battery modules is regular, that is the larger the thermal conductivity, the better the temperature control effect of the battery module, and the more uniform the temperature distribution. However, increasing the thermal conductivity of PCM will reduce its latent heat of phase change. By comparing the effects of different thermal conductivity on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, it can be concluded that when the thermal conductivity of PCM is greater than 2.5 W/(m·K), further increasing the thermal conductivity has little effect on the cooling effect of the battery module. Therefore, a PCM thermal conductivity of around 2.5 W/(m·K) is more suitable [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of latent heat of phase change on heat dissipation
ce:section.ce:para: The latent heat of phase transition refers to the heat absorbed or released when changing from one phase to another, so the latent heat of PCM phase transition determines how much battery heat can be absorbed when PCM melts. In order to explore the effect of latent heat of PCM phase transition on the heat dissipation performance of battery modules, six latent heat of phase transition (125 J/g, 150 J/g, 175 J/g, 200 J/g, 225 J/g and 250 J/g) are selected for simulation study. Fig. 14 shows the temperature distribution cloud diagram at the end of discharge of the battery module under different latent heat of phase transition.

It can be clearly seen from Fig. 14 that with the increase of latent heat of phase transition, the temperature of the battery module decreases, indicating that the phase change material with high latent heat will reduce the temperature rise of the battery module. Fig. 15 describes the liquid phase rate of PCM at the end of discharge of battery modules under different latent heat of phase transition. It can be seen that the larger the latent heat of phase transition of PCM, the smaller the liquid phase rate, indicating that the melting rate of PCM with high latent heat is slower. In summary, the effect of phase transition latent heat on the heat dissipation of battery modules can be obtained. The temperature of the battery module decreases with the increase of latent heat of phase transition, and the temperature uniformity of the battery module is better with the increase of latent heat of PCM. Therefore, weighing the latent heat of PCM phase transition and thermal conductivity, the range of 175 J/g∼200 J/g is more appropriate [22].

In view of the low thermal conductivity of PW, the high thermal conductivity carbon material EG was added to PW to improve the thermal conductivity of PCM, and the thermal parameters of the CPCM were tested and analyzed. The results show that the addition of EG can effectively improve the thermal conductivity of the CPCM, and the higher the proportion of EG content, the greater the thermal conductivity of EG/PW composite phase change materials, but the increase of EG content will reduce the phase change temperature and latent heat of EG/PW composite phase change materials. In order to study the temperature control effect of CPCM applied to battery thermal management, the battery module was assembled for experimental testing. The temperature values of the battery module in PCM cooling mode and natural convection cooling mode were compared to verify that the prepared CPCM had good heat dissipation effect when applied to the battery module. In addition, to study the performance of thermal properties parameters related to CPCM, a COMSOL numerical calculation model of battery module based on CPCM cooling and electrochemical thermal coupling model is established. The effectiveness and accuracy of the model are verified through heat generation experiments at different rates (0.5 C, 1 C, 1.5 C), with a maximum temperature error of 1.6 K and an error accuracy of less than 0.6%. Subsequently, comprehensively considering the maximum temperature, maximum temperature difference and PCM liquid phase rate of the battery module, the performance of phase change temperature, thermal conductivity and phase change latent heat is studied and reasonable value ranges are obtained, which are 312 K∼318 K, 2.5 W /(m·K), and 175 J/g∼200 J/g, respectively.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201426 and BK20210765), Taizhou Science and Technology Support project (TG202118) and Science and Technology Plan Project of Zhenjiang (CQ2022004).
